symptoms: Dark olive-green spots on leaves
Scabby lesions on fruits
Premature leaf drop
causes: Caused by the fungus Venturia inaequalis, spreads in cool, wet spring weather.
treatment: Apply Mancozeb 75% WP @ 2g/L during early spring
Remove and destroy fallen infected leaves
prevention: Plant resistant varieties like Red Delicious
Ensure proper spacing for air circulation
Apply preventive fungicide before bud break

symptoms: White powdery patches on leaves and shoots
Stunted shoot growth
Cracked fruits
causes: Podosphaera leucotricha fungus, thrives in high humidity.

symptoms: Rotting of corms from base
Yellowing of leaves
Foul smell from soil
Reduced flower production
causes: Caused by Fusarium solani. Thrives in waterlogged soils in Kashmir.
treatment: Treat corms with Carbendazim 50% WP @ 2g/L before planting
prevention: Rotate with non-host crops every 5 years

symptoms: Brown necrotic spots on leaf margins
Withered leaf tips
Premature foliage death
causes: Alternaria species, spread by wind and rain splashes.
prevention: Collect and burn infected plant debris

symptoms: Black spots on young fruits
Darkening of catkins
Leaf necrosis with dark margins
causes: Caused by Xanthomonas arboricola pv. juglandis. Prevalent during rainy seasons in hilly regions.
treatment: Spray Bordeaux mixture (1%) before and after flowering
prevention: Apply preventive copper spray in early spring

symptoms: Small dark spots on leaves
Premature dropping of leaves
Sunken spots on husks
causes: Ophiognomonia leptostyla fungus, overwinters in fallen leaves.
treatment: Apply Mancozeb @ 2.5g/L during early fruit development

symptoms: Yellow to white lesions along leaf veins
Wilting of seedlings
Grayish leaves in severe cases
causes: Caused by Xanthomonas oryzae. Spread by wind-driven rain and contaminated seeds.
treatment: Apply Streptocycline @ 0.5g + Copper Oxychloride @ 3g per liter
prevention: Use resistant varieties like Improved Samba Mahsuri
Clip tips of seedlings before transplanting

symptoms: Diamond-shaped lesions on leaves with gray centers
Brown spots on panicle branches
Collar rot
causes: Magnaporthe oryzae fungus, favored by high humidity and cool nights.

symptoms: Water-soaked oval spots on leaf sheaths near water level
Spots turn grayish-white with brown margins
causes: Rhizoctonia solani fungus.

symptoms: Orange-brown pustules on leaves
Powdery spores on leaf surface
Reduced grain filling
causes: Caused by Puccinia species. Spreads rapidly in warm, humid weather.

symptoms: A portion of the grain is converted into a black powdery mass
Fishy odor from infected grains
causes: Tilletia indica fungus, soil and seed-borne.
treatment: Seed treatment with Carboxin 75% WP @ 2.5g/kg seed

symptoms: Upward curling of leaves
Thickening of leaf veins
Stunted plant growth
Small or no bolls
causes: Transmitted by whitefly (Bemisia tabaci). Common in Punjab, Haryana, and Rajasthan.
prevention: Use yellow sticky traps for whitefly

symptoms: Angular, water-soaked leaf spots
Black arm (stem lesions)
Boll rot
causes: Xanthomonas axonopodis pv. malvacearum.

symptoms: Water-soaked dark spots on leaves
White mold on undersides
Brown rotting of tubers
causes: Caused by Phytophthora infestans. Thrives in cool, humid conditions.
treatment: Remove and burn infected plants immediately

symptoms: Target-like concentric brown spots on older leaves
Yellowing of surrounding leaf area
causes: Alternaria solani fungus.
prevention: Maintain plant vigor through proper fertilization

symptoms: Curling and crinkling of leaves
Stunted plant growth
Pale yellow leaves
Reduced fruit setting
causes: Tomato leaf curl virus, transmitted by whiteflies.
treatment: Uproot and bury infected plants immediately
prevention: Use nylon net (40 mesh) in nursery

symptoms: Dark, concentric spots on lower leaves
Fruit cracking and rotting at the top
Yellowing and dying of leaves
causes: Alternaria solani fungus.

symptoms: Vegetative shoots become bunched and misshapen
Inflorescences become thickened, shortened and produce no fruit
causes: Fusarium mangiferae fungus.
treatment: Prune and burn affected parts safely 30cm behind infection site

symptoms: Black irregular spots on leaves
Flower blight and drop
Black sunken necrotic spots on ripe fruits
causes: Colletotrichum gloeosporioides, favored by high humidity and rain.
treatment: Post-harvest hot water treatment (52°C for 10 min)

symptoms: Leaves are bunchy at the top
Narrow, stiff, and upright leaves
Dark green streaks on veins
Stunted growth
causes: Transmitted by the banana aphid (Pentalonia nigronervosa).
treatment: No cure; completely uproot and burn infected plants including rhizomes

symptoms: Yellowing of older leaves progressing upwards
Leaves collapse around the pseudostem (skirt appearance)
Brown discoloration of vascular tissues
causes: Fusarium oxysporum f. sp. cubense, a soil-borne fungus.
treatment: Capsule application of Carbendazim (50-60mg/capsule) into the corm
There is no complete cure; destroy infected plants
prevention: Plant resistant varieties (e.g., Grand Naine, Robusta)
Do not use suckers from infected areas

symptoms: Yellow mottling and distortion of leaves
Dark green rings on fruits
Water-soaked streaks on stems
causes: PRSV transmitted by aphids.
treatment: No cure. Completely destroy infected plants immediately
prevention: Avoid planting papaya near cucurbit crops

symptoms: Yellowing and dropping of leaves
Soft, foul-smelling rotting of the main root and lower stem
Sudden plant collapse
causes: Phytophthora palmivora or Pythium aphanidermatum fungi. Favored by waterlogged soil.
treatment: Drench soil around the base with Copper Oxychloride @ 3g/L or Metalaxyl @ 2g/L
prevention: Avoid water stagnation around the collar region

symptoms: Yellowing and drying of leaves top-down
Reddening of internal stem tissues with cross white patches
Alcoholic sour smell from split canes
causes: Colletotrichum falcatum, primarily seed-piece/set borne.
treatment: Uproot and burn diseased clumps immediately
Do not ration an infected crop
prevention: Use healthy seed setts treated with Carbendazim (0.1%) for 15 mins

symptoms: Production of a black whip-like structure from the central shoot
Profuse tillering
Narrow, upright leaves
causes: Sporisorium scitamineum fungus.
treatment: Rogue out and destroy infected whips inside a gunny bag to prevent spore spread
prevention: Hot water treatment of setts (50°C for 2 hours)

symptoms: Small water-soaked lesions that turn brown/purple
Yellow halo around the spots
Leaves dropping downwards and dying
causes: Alternaria porri, thrives in warm, humid weather.
treatment: Use a sticker with the fungicide for better adhesion

symptoms: Dark, slightly thickened spots on cotyledons and young leaves
Lesions rupture exposing black powdery spores
Stunted growth and bulb death
causes: Urocystis cepulae, a soil-borne fungus surviving for many years.
prevention: Grow onion plants from sets or transplants instead of direct seeding

symptoms: Curling and crinkling of leaves
Stunted, bushy plant appearance
Failure of flower and fruit setting
causes: Chilli leaf curl virus, transmitted by whiteflies and thrips.
treatment: No cure for the virus. Remove infected plants.
Control vectors vectors: Spray Imidacloprid @ 0.3ml/L for whiteflies or Spinosad @ 0.3ml/L for thrips

symptoms: Branches die back from the tips
Dark sunken spots on ripe fruits covered with pinkish concentric rings
Premature fruit drop
causes: Colletotrichum capsici. Prevalent in high humidity and warm temperatures.
treatment: Remove and burn diseased plant parts
prevention: Use disease-free seeds treated with Thiram
Avoid continuous cropping of chilli in the same field

symptoms: Dark brown circular spots with yellow halo (early)
Darker, irregular spots without halo on underside (late)
Severe defoliation resulting in low yield
causes: Cercospora arachidicola (early) and Phaeoisariopsis personata (late).
treatment: Spray Carbendazim @ 1g/L or Mancozeb @ 2.5g/L as soon as symptoms appear
prevention: Deep summer ploughing to destroy infected plant debris

symptoms: Orange/brown powdery pustules mostly on the underside of leaves
Leaves dry up and turn brown but stay attached to the stem
causes: Puccinia arachidis. Often occurs together with late leaf spot.
treatment: If both spot and rust exist, use Tebuconazole @ 1ml/L

symptoms: Yellowing of the entire network of veins in the leaf
Leaves become completely yellow if severe
Fruits become small, hard, and yellowish
causes: Transmitted exclusively by the whitefly (Bemisia tabaci).
treatment: Uproot and destroy infected plants immediately
Control whiteflies using Acetamiprid @ 0.4g/L or Neem Oil @ 5ml/L

symptoms: Large, elliptical, grayish-green or brown lesions on lower leaves
Lesions merge leading to complete blighting of leaves
causes: Exserohilum turcicum. Favored by mild temperatures and heavy dew.
treatment: Apply Propiconazole 25 EC @ 1ml/L if infection is severe before silking

symptoms: Systemic infection leads to chlorotic (yellow) streaks on leaves
White downy fungal growth on underside of leaves
Crazy top (leafy structures instead of tassels)
causes: Peronosclerospora species. Very damaging in young plants.
prevention: Seed treatment with Metalaxyl 35 WS @ 4g/kg seed
Sow before onset of heavy monsoons

symptoms: Yellow patches alternating with green patches on leaves
Rusty necrotic spots on older leaves
Reduced pod size and grain yield
causes: Mungbean yellow mosaic India virus (MYMIV), transmitted by whiteflies.
prevention: Grow resistant varieties designed for your zone

symptoms: Plants wilt and die prematurely
Tiny black specks (microsclerotia) visible when the lower stem bark is peeled away
Root decay
causes: Macrophomina phaseolina. Disease is triggered by hot, dry weather stress.
treatment: No fungicide cures it once established. Drench surrounding areas with Carbendazim if necessary
prevention: Conserve soil moisture through irrigation during dry spells

symptoms: Drooping of upper leaves
Yellowing and drying of leaves top-down
Black discoloration of internal root tissues when split open
causes: Fusarium oxysporum f. sp. ciceris. Highly destructive soil-borne fungus.
treatment: Pull out and destroy wilting plants
Once established in the field, chemical control is ineffective
prevention: Seed treatment with Trichoderma viride @ 4g/kg seed + Vitavax @ 1g/kg
Grow resistant varieties like JG 11 or Digvijay

symptoms: Circular spots on leaves and pods with dark margins and gray centers
Concentric rings of tiny black dots inside the spots
Stems may break at lesion points
causes: Ascochyta rabiei. Favored by high rainfall and cool weather.
prevention: Delay sowing to avoid cool, wet weather during early growth

symptoms: Sudden wilting of green foliage without yellowing
Brown vascular discoloration in stem
White bacterial ooze when cut stem is placed in water
causes: Ralstonia solanacearum. Highly destructive soil-borne bacteria.
treatment: Apply bleaching powder to infected soil spots
prevention: Avoid planting in infested fields for at least 3 years

symptoms: Stunting of plants
Yellow to orange leaf discoloration starting from the tip
Reduced tillering
Delayed flowering or empty panicles
causes: Caused by RTBV and RTSV viruses, transmitted by Green Leafhoppers (GLH).
treatment: Control leafhoppers with Buprofezin or Imidacloprid
prevention: Adjust planting times to avoid peak GLH populations

symptoms: Yellowish, oily spots on upper leaf surface
White, cottony growth on the underside corresponding to the spots
Berries covered with white fungal growth, later turning brown, shriveling, and dropping
causes: Plasmopara viticola. Thrives in warm, wet conditions.
treatment: Ensure thorough coverage of foliage and bunches
prevention: Prune vines to improve air circulation and sunlight penetration
Apply preventive copper sprays before the onset of rains

symptoms: Ashy white powdery patches on leaves, stems, and berries
Leaves turn yellow and curl upwards
Berries remain small, hard, and may crack open, emitting a musty odor
causes: Erysiphe necator. Favored by warm, dry weather with high humidity, even without rain.
treatment: Spray systemic fungicides like Myclobutanil @ 0.4g/L or Penconazole @ 0.5ml/L
prevention: Apply preventive sulphur during early growth stages

symptoms: Raised, corky, brownish lesions with a yellow halo on leaves, stems, and fruits
Lesions are crater-like and rough to the touch
Premature fruit drop and dieback of twigs
causes: Xanthomonas axonopodis pv. citri. Spread by rain splash, wind, and contaminated tools.
treatment: Prune all infected twigs before monsoon
Spray Streptomycin @ 100ppm combined with Copper Oxychloride (0.3%) 2-3 times at 15-day intervals
prevention: Control leaf miners which create points of entry for the bacteria

symptoms: Small yellow spots on leaves that enlarge to form brown streaks with yellow halos
Center of spots turns grayish with a black border
Premature ripening of bunches
causes: Mycosphaerella fijiensis (Black Sigatoka) or M. musicola (Yellow Sigatoka).
treatment: Apply Mancozeb @ 2.5g/L as a protectant fungicide
prevention: Remove and safely destroy heavily infected leaves (de-leafing)
Improve drainage to reduce field humidity

symptoms: Yellowing and drooping of leaves, starting from the lower ones and progressing upwards
Brown or black discoloration in the woody tissues of the stem when split open
Complete drying of the plant
causes: Fusarium oxysporum f.sp. vasinfectum. Soil-borne fungus.
treatment: Chemical control is generally ineffective in established infections
Spot drenching with Carbendazim (0.1%) may help limit spread
prevention: Deep ploughing in summer to expose soil to heat
Crop rotation with non-host crops like cereals

symptoms: Clusters of small greenish-yellow insects on stems, leaves, and inflorescences
Curling and yellowing of leaves
Stunted growth and honeydew secretion leading to sooty mold
causes: Lipaphis erysimi. Infestation is severe when weather is cloudy and humid.
treatment: Spray Imidacloprid 17.8 SL @ 0.5ml/L or Dimethoate 30 EC @ 2ml/L
Wait for 15 days before harvesting if spraying late
prevention: Timely sowing (e.g., in October in North India) reduces aphid attack
Conserve natural enemies like ladybird beetles
Pluck and destroy early infested twigs

symptoms: White, raised, blister-like pustules on the lower surface of leaves
Swelling and distortion of stems and floral parts (staghead formation)
causes: Albugo candida. Favored by cool, moist weather.
treatment: Spray Ridomil MZ (Metalaxyl + Mancozeb) @ 2g/L when symptoms appear, repeat after 10-15 days
prevention: Destroy weeds of the mustard family

symptoms: Dark brown, circular spots with pale centers on leaves
Stem lesions leading to plant collapse
Rotting of fruits with black pycnidia (pimples) on the surface
causes: Phomopsis vexans. Seed, soil, and debris borne.
treatment: Spray Mancozeb @ 2.5g/L or Carbendazim @ 1g/L at fruiting stage

symptoms: Extreme reduction in leaf size
Leaves become narrow, soft, and pale green or yellow
Stunted, bushy plant growth
Flowers converted to leaf-like structures, no fruiting
causes: Phytoplasma, transmitted by the leafhopper (Hishimonus phycitis).
treatment: No cure for infected plants; they must be immediately uprooted and destroyed
Control the leafhopper vector by spraying Imidacloprid @ 0.5ml/L or Thiamethoxam @ 0.3g/L

symptoms: V-shaped yellow or brown lesions extending inward from leaf margins
Blackening of veins within lesions
Dark rings visible in the stem cross-section
causes: Xanthomonas campestris pv. campestris. Spreads rapidly in warm, wet conditions.
treatment: Seed treatment with hot water (50°C for 25 mins) followed by Streptocycline (100 ppm)
prevention: Ensure crop rotation of at least 3 years with non-cruciferous crops
